sales.title:
Seleukis and Pieria, Seleukeia and Antioch, Æ14
ANCIENT GREECE. SELEUKIS AND PIERIA, SELEUKEIA AND ANTIOCH.
Bronze AE14, circa 150-145 BC.
Adelphoi Demi issue. Obv: jugate bearded heads of the Demoi of Seleukeia and Antioch right, wearing tainiai. Rev: thunderbolt, AΔEΛΦΩN above, ΔHMΩN and uncertain monogram below; all within wreath.

In secure plastic holder, graded NGC Graded AU Strike: 4/5, Surface: 4/5 Fine Style, certification number 2164075-001.

Possibly Unique: seemingly unpublished in the standard references and no other examples on CoinArchives.

A fascinating issue, the Adelphoi Demi coins have sparked a great deal of debate, largely centred on who exactly these 'brother peoples' were who were responsible for their minting. The traditional view, that these coins were issued jointly from a tetrarchy of the cities of Antioch on the Orontes, Apameia on the Orontes, Laodikeia ad Mare and Seleukeia Pieria, followed from Strabo's statement that these cities 'were called sisters (adelphai) because of the concord (homonoia) between them' (16.2.4). The consensus now, however, is that this issue was released by the cities of Antioch and Seleukeia only, in alliance: examples of this coinage have been found only in these two cities; for the most part, the 'typology of the series is entirely drawn from the regular numismatic iconography' of the two (HGC 9, p.293); and the series' Demos heads - on coins such as ours - depict only two 'brother peoples' conjoined rather than four.

This series is interesting for a number of other reasons. It contains one of the earliest personifications of the Demos (the 'People') in the numismatic record; it looks forward to the Roman Imperial period, when such 'alliance' (homonoia) coinages between cities were common, particularly in the Greek East; and it speaks to the strength and rulership of the Seleukid kings, in particular Alexander I Balas, under whose reign this issue was produced - for example, there has been debate as to whether the existence of city-based alliances within the Kingdom implies that the relative power of the Seleukid overlords was waning.

We are proud to add a further type to our knowledge of such a numismatically important series with this lot, which combines the heads of the Demoi with the thunderbolt motif usually found only in conjunction with the head of Zeus (HGC 9-1396).

Reference: Seemingly unpublished in the standard references: BMC-; HGC-; SNG Copenhagen-; Mionnet-; for obv., cf. HGC 6-1395; for rev., cf. HGC 6-1396.
Weight: 8.24 g.
Unpublished & Possibly Unique
